countries: Australia (Great Barrier Reef, Lord Howe Island)
nativeRange.habitat: Adults frequent lagoon and seaward reefs with hard substrate at depths of roughly 0–90 m; juveniles are often abundant in tide pools and shallow inshore areas, sometimes near freshwater runoffs

decorPreferences: ample open swimming space along the length of the tank
rockwork crevices and caves for shelter
long footprint (6 ft+) preferred over tall depth
tightly fitted lid recommended when mixed with other species

diet.notes: Primarily grazes filamentous and macroalgae from live rock; dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been documented accepting flake/pellet foods and small meaty items such as mysis shrimp in mixed-community feedings. Frequent algae offerings have been reported to strengthen immune response and reduce conspecific aggression.

avoidWith: established conspecifics added later (introduce a small group simultaneously)
very aggressive tankmates that out-compete it for food

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x801. Large sharp shoal of Convict Tangs showing the six black prison-bars on silvery bodies — best represents this famously shoaling reef surgeonfish.
brian.gratwicke; 4159x2773. Tack-sharp single specimen, whole body in frame, vivid yellow dorsal and crisp six black bars — cleanest showcase of diagnostic features.
Rickard Zerpe; 1759x1319. Sharp in-water lateral view of a lone vibrant individual; adds a natural-habitat angle for variety.

summary: The Convict Tang is a silvery Indo-Pacific surgeonfish marked by six vertical black bars — the prison-stripe pattern behind its common name. Generally considered an active grazer, it is best suited to mature reef systems with generous swimming space and a herbivore-heavy diet.
careNotes:
The Convict Tang (Acanthurus triostegus) is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific and into the Eastern Pacific. Adults tend to reach roughly 20–27 cm in well-kept aquariums, with a recorded maximum of 27 cm.

This is an active, open-water swimmer that covers considerable ground in the wild, and the aquarium should reflect that. A tank of at least 700 L with a long footprint — 180 cm (6 ft) or more — is generally advised to give the fish adequate room to move. Plenty of live rock provides natural grazing surfaces, but open swimming lanes along the length of the tank are equally important. A tightly fitted lid is recommended, as this species has a moderate escape risk in mixed setups.

Water quality should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef parameters: specific gravity 1.020–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, and temperature 23–27°C.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Offering dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week is generally advised, and the species has been documented accepting flake, pellet, and small meaty items such as mysis shrimp during mixed-community feedings. Frequent algae offerings have been reported to strengthen immune response and reduce aggression among tankmates. Like many surgeonfishes, the Convict Tang is susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition where the skin along the lateral line and face degrades, typically associated with poor water quality or a vitamin-deficient diet. A varied, algae-rich diet and consistent water quality tend to reduce this risk.

The Convict Tang is generally considered reef-safe; well-fed individuals tend not to nip at corals, though occasional sampling has been reported when algae is scarce. Temperament is best described as semi-aggressive: generally peaceable with non-tang reef fish once established, but commonly antagonistic toward other Acanthurus species and similarly shaped surgeonfishes. Multiple Convict Tangs can sometimes coexist if a small group is introduced simultaneously into a sufficiently large system, rather than adding individuals to a tank where one is already established.

Like all surgeonfishes, the Convict Tang possesses a sharp, blade-like spine at the base of the tail (the caudal-peduncle scalpel) that is used in defense and during conflicts with rival fish. The spine is not venomous, but it can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates. Care should be taken when netting or transferring the fish.
breedingNotes: The Convict Tang has not been bred in captivity. In the wild, Acanthurus triostegus reproduces through broadcast-spawning aggregations in which males and females release eggs and sperm into open water. Sexual maturity is reached at around 8.8 cm. The resulting larvae pass through an extended planktonic stage (known as the acronurus phase) before settling onto reefs, and this combination of aggregation-spawning behaviour and a long larval development period makes captive breeding impractical in typical home aquaria.

additional.name: Reef Chasers Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~8 in), minimum tank (125 gal / 473 L) with wider-than-tall footprint, semi-aggressive toward other tangs, caudal-spine defense, HLLE susceptibility, herbivorous grazing on live-rock algae supplemented with nori/leafy greens, netted-lid recommendation in mixed setups.

fish.breedingNotes: Not documented in captivity. In the wild, populations form broadcast-spawning aggregations producing pelagic eggs; sexual maturity is reached at around 8.8 cm. The extended planktonic acronurus larval stage and aggregation-spawning behaviour make captive breeding impractical in typical home aquaria.
